Biocomposite kitchenware · with Yellon

Kiaro

Can liquid wood feel premium?

←Back to work

A six-piece kitchenware collection in Arboblend, a lignin-based biocomposite. A bachelor thesis with Vaida Staberg, developed together with Yellon.

RoleShared project. I drove CAD, form development and rendering.
PartnerYellon
MethodDouble Diamond
RecognitionÖhrskog Foundation scholarship 2026
The challenge

Making a "cheap" material read as premium.

Arboblend costs more than ordinary plastic, so it lands in a higher price bracket. But people don't read biodegradable material as premium. They park it somewhere neutral, and when they buy, function matters more than sustainability. The problem wasn't the material. It was the perception.

What the survey said · n=100 ×2
81%ranked function highest, above aesthetics and sustainability.
38%read biodegradable material as premium. Most saw it as neutral.
4form strategies tested on the same servers, identical dimensions.
Process

Same object, four forms.

Double Diamond, but material-driven: the material set the direction, not a user problem. After a material study and a survey of 100 people, I built four versions of the same salad servers. Identical base dimensions, only the radii, taper and handle profile changed. A validation survey then ranked them on perceived premium. Concept C sat at the top, though the margins were small and premium turned out to shift with age and gender.

The form language

Five principles carried the series.

  1. 01Reduced form
  2. 02Balanced proportions
  3. 03Low visual complexity
  4. 04Matte, lightly textured surface
  5. 05Material honesty
The series

Six pieces.

Kitchen tools, storage and serving. The three categories the material study showed people accepted most.

Reflection

Sustainability alone doesn't sell premium. Form does. Small changes in radii and proportion moved how premium a product felt, even with the material and function held constant. And premium isn't universal, it depends on who's looking.
